topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, nauseating knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly as I scanned the header. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My meticulously researched piece, the one I’d poured weeks of late nights into, was attributed to some complete stranger. A cold, hard wave washed over me, quickly followed by a searing heat that bloomed in my chest. I reread the text, my eyes darting back and forth, searching for any misplaced word, any sign of tampering. When I finally found the damning evidence, the air seemed to evacuate the room. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I stood up, pacing the small space like a caged animal, my jaw tight enough to crack. I needed to know who had done this, and I needed them to feel the full weight of what they had done.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the blue light reflecting off my wide, unblinking eyes. It wasn't a typo; it was a complete fabrication. My article, my voice, was being used by someone else entirely. A slow, burning fury began to build, starting low in my gut and rising steadily until it felt like molten metal pressing against my sternum. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning beneath me as I pushed myself away from the desk. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white. "This is unacceptable," I muttered, the words sounding thin and brittle even to my own ears. I began to rapidly cycle through my contacts, my breathing becoming shallow and quick. Every attempt to rationalize the situation dissolved under the sheer audacity of the deception. I felt a desperate need to confront whoever had stolen my work, to demand an explanation that would satisfy the churning dissatisfaction inside me.

[story 3]

When the notification finally arrived, I felt a strange, dizzying disconnect. I read the attribution, and the world tilted. A slow, deep rumble started in my chest, a vibration that seemed to shake my very bones. I sat motionless for a long moment, the silence of the office amplifying the frantic hammering of my heart. Then, the control I usually maintained evaporated. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. My voice, when I finally spoke, was dangerously low, laced with a sharp, cutting edge. "How dare they?" I whispered, the disbelief quickly morphing into a fierce, protective anger. I walked over to the window, gripping the sill until my fingers ached. The view outside seemed distant and irrelevant; all that mattered was the injustice of it, the sheer disrespect shown to my labor. I turned back, my expression set in a hard mask of absolute condemnation.
